startScreenOptions.title: AN8.1-AN8.4 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN8.1-AN8.4.

interactions.label: In a displaced clavicle fracture at the junction of the medial two-thirds and la

params.question: In a displaced clavicle fracture at the junction of the medial two-thirds and lateral one-third, which pair of forces best explains the typical displacement pattern?

answers.text: Trapezius elevates the lateral fragment; deltoid depresses the medial fragment

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Trapezius attaches to the lateral third and spine of the scapula, not to the medial fragment. The muscle responsible for elevating the medial fragment is the sternocleidomastoid, which inserts on the medial third of the clavicle.

answers.text: Sternocleidomastoid pulls the medial fragment superiorly; weight of the arm pulls the lateral fragment inferiorly

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The sternocleidomastoid elevates the medial fragment while gravity acting through the unsupported arm depresses the lateral fragment, producing the characteristic step deformity.

answers.text: Subclavius pulls both fragments inferiorly towards the first rib

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Subclavius lies in the subclavian groove on the inferior surface and protects the subclavian vessels, but it does not produce the characteristic upward displacement of the medial fragment seen in clavicle fractures.

answers.text: Pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment anteriorly; serratus anterior pulls the medial fragment posteriorly

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The characteristic displacement in a clavicle fracture is vertical, not anteroposterior. The sternocleidomastoid elevates the medial fragment and the weight of the arm depresses the lateral fragment.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key forces are the sternocleidomastoid (pulling the medial fragment up) and gravity acting through the arm (pulling the lateral fragment down). This creates a step deformity and places the subclavian vessels and brachial plexus at risk.

Well done! The sternocleidomastoid pulls the medial fragment superiorly while the weight of the arm depresses the lateral fragment. In displaced fractures, the subclavian vessels and brachial plexus are at risk of injury.

interactions.label: A fracture at the surgical neck of the humerus is most likely to injure the radi

params.question: A fracture at the surgical neck of the humerus is most likely to injure the radial nerve.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: The radial nerve lies in the spiral groove on the mid-shaft of the humerus, not at the surgical neck. A surgical neck fracture endangers the axillary nerve instead. The key association is: surgical neck → axillary nerve; mid-shaft spiral groove → radial nerve (wrist drop).
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Well done! The surgical neck fracture endangers the axillary nerve, not the radial nerve. The radial nerve runs in the spiral groove on the posterior mid-shaft, where its injury produces wrist drop. Remember: surgical neck → axillary nerve; mid-shaft → radial nerve.

interactions.label: A 20-year-old student falls on an outstretched hand and an MRI confirms a scapho

params.question: A 20-year-old student falls on an outstretched hand and an MRI confirms a scaphoid fracture. Why is this fracture at particular risk of avascular necrosis?

answers.text: The scaphoid is entirely surrounded by synovial fluid, which prevents any periosteal blood supply

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While the scaphoid does lie within the wrist joint, the primary reason for its vulnerable blood supply is that most of its surface is covered by articular cartilage, severely restricting the areas where blood vessels can enter the bone.

answers.text: Most of the scaphoid surface is covered by articular cartilage, severely limiting blood vessel entry

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The extensive articular cartilage covering leaves very few areas for blood vessels to penetrate the bone. This results in a precarious, largely retrograde blood supply that fractures can easily disrupt.

answers.text: The scaphoid receives its blood supply exclusively from the ulnar artery

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The scaphoid receives blood supply primarily from branches of the radial artery, not the ulnar artery. Its vulnerability to avascular necrosis stems from the extensive articular cartilage covering that limits blood vessel entry points.

answers.text: The scaphoid is the smallest carpal bone and lacks sufficient surface for nutrient foramina

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The scaphoid is actually the largest bone in the proximal carpal row. Its risk of avascular necrosis comes from most of its surface being articular cartilage, not from its size.

overallFeedback.feedback: Most of the scaphoid surface is covered by articular cartilage, leaving very few entry points for blood vessels. This limited, retrograde blood supply is why scaphoid fractures are prone to avascular necrosis — and why the medical officer in our opening scenario immobilised the wrist immediately, even before confirming the fracture on MRI.

Correct! The extensive articular cartilage covering of the scaphoid restricts blood vessel entry, resulting in a precarious blood supply. This is why scaphoid fractures — especially through the waist — are prone to avascular necrosis and require early immobilisation even when the initial X-ray appears normal.
